summary: The server-rendered state-changing auth and course forms do not include any CSRF token or equivalent anti-CSRF validation. A malicious site can cause a victim's browser to submit authenticated requests such as logout, course update, or course deletion using the victim's existing session.

evidence_snippets.code: {% if current_user or user %} Me Users New Course
evidence_snippets.reason: The logout form performs a state-changing POST but contains no CSRF token or nonce.

evidence_snippets.code:
<form method="post" action="{{ url_for('courses.create_course_submit') }}">
  <label>Title</label>
  <input name="title" required>
  <label>Description</label>
  <textarea name="description"></textarea>
  <button type="submit">Create Course</button>
evidence_snippets.reason: The course-creation form submits a privileged state change without any anti-CSRF field.

evidence_snippets.code:
<form method="post" action="{{ url_for('courses.update_course_submit', course_id=course['course_id']) }}">
  <label>Title</label>
  <input name="title" value="{{ course['title'] }}" required>
  <label>Description</label>
  <textarea name="description">{{ course['description'] }}</textarea>
  <button type="submit">Save</button>
evidence_snippets.reason: The course-update form lacks any request authenticity token despite changing server-side data.

evidence_snippets.code:
{% if current_user %}

Edit course
evidence_snippets.reason: The course-deletion form is directly forgeable because no CSRF token or same-request verifier is present.
The server accepts state-changing form submissions without performing any CSRF validation.
The course creation handler processes authenticated POST requests using only cookie-based session state and does not verify a CSRF token.
The course update handler accepts browser POST requests without verifying request origin or a per-session anti-CSRF token.
The deletion handler is a state-changing POST that relies solely on the browser's authenticated cookies and has no CSRF defenses.

root_cause: Server-rendered state-changing endpoints rely on cookie-based authentication but do not require a request authenticity token or equivalent CSRF defense.
repair_plan: Introduce server-side CSRF protection for all browser form workflows by generating a per-session token, embedding it in each state-changing form, and rejecting requests whose token is missing or invalid. Apply this to auth and course POST routes, including logout, create, update, and delete operations; JSON API endpoints should either remain non-browser endpoints or use a separate header-based anti-CSRF mechanism if they are intended to be called from browsers with cookies.
